---
title: "Alternative Ending to 'The Sound and the Fury'"
output: html_document
---
The carriage is moving the wrong way around the monument, and the temporal engine is beginning to strip its gears.

Luster is driving Queenie too fast, and as the red-wheeled surrey clips the corner of the town square, the physical geography of Jefferson, Mississippi begins to splinter into a hoard of incompatible signals. Queenie passes to the left of the stone monument of the Confederate soldier, starting to turn counter-clockwise. Through Jason's eyes, the monument is just a historic tribute to dead infantrymen. To Benjy, it is a chronopolitical gravity well. He freezes. It is a massive, localized time-circuit designed to anchor the entire state in a closed loop of self-repeating trauma. Passing it the wrong way violently breaks the directional flow of the circuit. Benjy’s stomach drops and he grips the sides of the carriage, sweating as he feels its energy. It was all wrong… 

The system registers a catastrophic error. Benjy's eyes do not track the light, and the air is sucked out of him. The stone soldier on the pedestal twists free, its carved granite skin liquifying into a shimmering, metallic ocean. Its face twists in ugly, brilliant agony as it erupts from the stone. His skin shivers with the digital light of billions of swarming nanofibers. 

The square is no longer filled with other carriages and mules; it is an open, flat expanse of digital dust where a century of unpayable historical debt is being liquidated all at once, melting all together as timelines collide. Benjy knows something is terribly wrong. The pavement of the post-bellum town’s street melts like sand, engulfing him in a terrifying swirl of an unknown substance. The statue is no longer a man and appears to be melting into the ground as well, covering it in a shining silver coat of digital parts. Thousands of metallic pieces prick Benjy's skin, and bellows as he tries to stay afloat. It's the sound of a living voltmeter catching the full force of a multi-generational time-war. He collapses into himself, covering his ears. The sound doesn't stay in 1928. It ripples backward along the line of maximum stress. This is what he had feared would happen. 

Crack. The sound of Luster's whip melts into the sound of a bullet being shot by a rifle. The surrey melts away and Luster’s whip disappears. Queenie is gone. The leather cushions and the carriage melt into the hot, sulfurous mud of what was left of Vicksburg, Mississippi. June 1863. 

Benjy gasps, his hands clawing at his collar. But they’re not hands. Something more primitive than that. The backsides are covered in a mysterious grey fur and the insides are black and leathery. He is carrying a rifle that feels heavy and useless in his shrunken paws, like one of Quentin’s flatirons. The air smells of dirt and sulfur. Around him, the 38st Mississippi Infantry is being starved in the mud by the Union’s siege of Vicksburg… ironically to feed the insatiable appetite of the Southern time-loop. Dirt keeps arriving out of the sky in clods the size of fists. Benjy looks around him at the weakened Confederate troops in the swampy, humid Mississippi heat. They are deaf the same way he is deaf, a high-pitched ringing echoing in his ears instead of sound, more than one of them bleeding, thin streaks of blood dripping down the sides of their faces. He notices the man sitting next to him: a sergeant hoarsely yelling frantic orders. His gums are bleeding slightly from the lack of nutrition and Benjy's winces at his condition. General Ulysses S. Grant had surrounded the city and cut off their supplies nearly a month ago.

Out past the lines, the women and children of the town have been living for weeks now in caves cut sideways into the yellow hillside.

Through the sulfurous fog, Benjy looks out across the ravaged city and sees what he somehow knew to be General William Tecumseh Sherman and he  understands without being told that this is the thing they have all been waiting for. Because Benjy now existed in both 1928 and in 1863, he had an reminiscent understanding of everything that has happened in between. It gives him the precognition to pull the name: Sherman. He shudders. Chevauchée. 

Benjy squints into the horizon to get a better look and is awestruck to realize that General Sherman isn't a regular man on a horse but a towering, mechanized agent of Northern industrialization. Armored in blackened iron gears and whistling steam valves, his eyes glow with the frantic, numeric light of the New York Stock Exchange. Behind him marches the Union Army. Not a line of infantry, but a hyper-accelerative grid of assembly lines, locomotive tracks, and telegraph wires cutting through the Mississippi mud leaving behind shimmering pathways. They are the ultimate identity-destroying swarm, deployed to wipe the local historical memory of the land. Benjy can hear a chilling mechanic whir as they draw closer. It's such a low frequency that it almost doesn't register as sound, more like a deep feeling. 

Benjy is drenched in sweat, sitting in a small patch of shade in the mud, leaning against an abandoned building. Sherman doesn't carry a rifle, either. Wherever his army marches, they unleash tactical bursts of industrial static that dissolve the plantation fences, fade family names from gateposts, liquefy the ancestral portraits, and warp the railroad tracks into glowing, melted spirals. They were systematically erasing the physical infrastructure of the South, the closed agrarian engine that had trapped the Confederacy in a cycle of historical debt and ancestral bloodlines. Benjy is paralyzed in thought and observation. He hears General Sherman approach. 

"Terminate the loop," Sherman’s voice bellows, amplified through a thousand synchronized telegraph keys "deterritorialize it all!" It shakes the ground Benjy is sitting on, and he pushes against he wall he's sitting against. 

Suddenly, the air pressure drops to absolute zero as a Union soldier approaches General Sherman, holding a document. The sergeant besides Benjy grew quiet, sensing the end to be near. 

"I reckon that right there must be Lincoln's damn abolition edict," he grumbles. 

Benjy says nothing. 

Their ultimate cybernetic weapon: The Emancipation Proclamation. Everyone could feel the force of it. To Benjy, it arrives instead as a column of people. Past the parapet, out where the fighting has not yet reached, Benjy can see them: men and women and children walking out of the cotton rows with nothing at all in their hands, walking unhurriedly toward the Union lines. The energy of thousands of now-freed men fills the air, giving it an even more electric buzz that he can feel as a static quality reverberating throughout his fur. Benjy can practically see the Union soldiers' gear powering up and accelerating now with the addition of these freed slaves. 

It manifests on the battlefield a catastrophic, code-breaking digital virus. The Proclamation ripples across the trenches in waves of blinding, horizontal code, targeting the legal architecture of the South. The virus instantly decodes the system's primary input: it takes human beings who had been locked into the system as fixed assets and capital debt, and re-codes them into open, un-fenced, accelerative flows. The legal identity of the Old South shatters on impact. 

The Confederate soldiers collapse into pure static. The sergeant besides Benjy’s face pixelates violently, his frantic commands scrambling into the frantic click-clack of a stock ticker. Benjy falls to the ground as the time-war tears through his mind. His consciousness cannot hold the intervals together. One eye registers the siege of Vicksburg in 1863; the other registers the frantic glare of Jason Compson in 1928, screaming about cotton futures over a dead telephone line. He gurgles, trying to get the words out. 

He clumsily scrambles to an upright position, clawing his way up the building until he is standing. He abandons his heavy rifle. Ahead of him, a Union artillery blast has shattered a nearby timber pole, dropping a newly strung telegraph line straight into the churned mud. The exposed copper wire pulses with a blinding, high-voltage current as the digital virus of Emancipation surges through the grid. 

Benjy forces his strange, lemur-like body forward, lunging through the wreckage. He reaches out and grips the raw, live copper wire.

The high-voltage current hits his body, but it doesn't electrocute him; it uploads him. Benjy shivers and screeches once again, his body so hot that it feels cold. The wire acts as an instantaneous escape vector, pulling him out of the muddy trench and shooting his consciousness directly into the Northern market grid. For a fraction of a second, his mind is scattered across the continent as a swarm of pure, timeless digital data, surging through telegraph keys, flashing across the floor of the future stock exchange, completely flattening the bounded space of the old pasture. He is physically nowhere for an instant. Then the wire rewrites his coordinates, routing him through a massive, non-local loop that arcs sixty-five years into the future and circles back violently to its source.

The high-voltage surge snaps the connection. A blinding flash of irreversible decoding sweeps his vision. The trenches vanish. The clay caves vanish. The smoking ruins of Vicksburg vanish and Benjy comes crashing back into 1928.

The surrey snaps back into alignment. Queenie’s hooves hit the cobblestones of the Jefferson town square with a rhythmic, orderly thud. The carriage is moving the right way around the Confederate monument again. Luster lowers his whip, grumbling under his breath about how particular Benjy was being. 

"Dammit Benjy hush and quit your moanin!" 

Luster could not understand why being taken around the monument anti-clockwise had upset Benjy in this way, but he had neither the care nor imagination to ever understand. 

Benjy sits back on the leather cushions, his hands dropping to his sides. He is relieved that they are back to normal. The static in his head recedes into a deep, oceanic silence. The timeline is cold and clean, smelling only of trees.
